items.title: Identify domain controllers and naming contexts
items.description: Locate all domain controllers via DNS SRV records (_ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs), LDAP rootDSE, and nltest. Capture the forest root, domain DN, and configuration/schema naming contexts to scope the rest of enumeration. Informational finding useful for hardening and compliance alignment. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You can list every DC FQDN/IP, the domain and forest DNS names, the base DN, and the forest functional level. Common tags: recon, ldap, dns.
items.how_to_find: You can list every DC FQDN/IP, the domain and forest DNS names, the base DN, and the forest functional level.
items.how_to_fix: Remediate "Identify domain controllers and naming contexts" by applying vendor and CIS/OWASP hardening guidance for Enumeration — Domain & Forest Recon. Implement least privilege, enable required logging, validate in staging, then re-scan with the same tools. Track ownership and set a remediation date based on severity.

items.title: Enumerate users, groups, and computers
items.description: Pull the full directory of user accounts, security groups (especially privileged groups like Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and protected groups), and computer objects via authenticated or anonymous LDAP. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You have a deduplicated list of enabled users, group memberships for privileged groups, and computer objects with OS versions. Common tags: recon, ldap, users.

Implement least privilege, enable required logging, validate in staging, then re-scan with the same tools. Track ownership and set a remediation date based on severity.

items.title: Retrieve domain password and lockout policy
items.description: Read the domain password policy and any fine-grained password policies (PSOs) to determine minimum length, complexity, lockout threshold, and observation window. This directly informs safe password spraying. Informational finding useful for hardening and compliance alignment. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You know the lockout threshold, lockout duration, observation window, and minimum password length before attempting any guessing. Common tags: recon, policy, spray-prep.

items.title: Discover SPNs and service accounts
items.description: Enumerate accounts with servicePrincipalName values set. These are candidates for Kerberoasting and reveal the service surface (SQL, web, HTTP, MSSQL) running under domain accounts. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You have a list of user accounts with SPNs, flagged separately from machine-account SPNs. Common tags: recon, kerberos, spn.

items.title: Flag accounts with delegation and risky UAC attributes
items.description: Identify accounts trusted for unconstrained/constrained delegation, accounts with DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH (AS-REP roastable), disabled-but-present accounts, and accounts with adminCount=1 protected by AdminSDHolder. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You have lists of unconstrained/constrained delegation principals, preauth-not-required users, and adminCount=1 objects. items.title: Map domain and forest trusts
items.description: Enumerate inbound/outbound trusts, trust direction, transitivity, and trust type (parent-child, external, forest). Trust mapping reveals lateral and cross-forest attack paths and SID filtering posture. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You have a trust diagram with direction, transitivity, and SID-filtering status for each trust relationship. items.description: Collect sessions, ACLs, group memberships, trusts, GPOs, and local admin data using SharpHound (Windows), the Python collector (bloodhound-python), or netexec BloodHound module. Choose collection methods (DCOnly, All, LoggedOn) based on stealth requirements. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: A complete BloodHound zip imports without errors and the graph shows users, computers, groups, and session/ACL edges. items.title: Recover GPP cpassword credentials
items.description: Search SYSVOL Groups.xml and other Group Policy Preferences files for cpassword fields. The AES key is published by Microsoft, so any cpassword can be decrypted to plaintext, often yielding local admin passwords. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Any cpassword found is decrypted to a usable plaintext credential and validated against a host. Common tags: gpp, cpassword, sysvol.

items.description: Read logon scripts, batch files, and PowerShell in SYSVOL/NETLOGON for hardcoded credentials, mapped-drive passwords, and references to internal hosts and service accounts. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Scripts are reviewed and any embedded credentials or sensitive hostnames are extracted and tested. items.title: Check for DCSync replication rights
items.description: Identify non-DC principals granted DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All on the domain object. These extended rights enable a full DCSync of credential material. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You can name every principal (outside expected DCs/admins) that holds both replication extended rights. Common tags: acl, dcsync, replication.

items.description: Find principals able to read ms-Mcs-AdmPwd (legacy LAPS) or the encrypted LAPS attributes. Reading LAPS yields local admin passwords for managed machines. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. items.description: Identify principals with write access to the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute of target objects. This enables Shadow Credentials attacks to authenticate as the target via PKINIT. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You can name principals able to write msDS-KeyCredentialLink on users/computers and the resulting impersonation targets. items.title: Request TGS tickets for SPN accounts
items.description: Enumerate user accounts with SPNs and request TGS-REP tickets for each. The encrypted ticket portion is derived from the service account password and can be cracked offline. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You have extracted crackable TGS hashes (krb5tgs format) for one or more SPN-enabled user accounts. Common tags: kerberos, kerberoast, spn.

Implement least privilege, enable required logging, validate in staging, then re-scan with the same tools. Track ownership and set a remediation date based on severity.

items.title: Force RC4 (etype 23) downgrade where possible
items.description: Where supported, request tickets with RC4 encryption to obtain faster-cracking hashes. Note that AES-only accounts yield etype 17/18 hashes that crack more slowly. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You record the encryption type of each ticket and prioritize RC4 hashes for cracking. Common tags: kerberos, rc4, kerberoast.

items.title: Crack TGS hashes offline
items.description: Run the extracted krb5tgs hashes against wordlists and rules with hashcat/john. Service accounts often have weak, non-rotated passwords, making this highly effective. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: At least one service account password is recovered, or the wordlist/rule strategy is documented as exhausted. Common tags: kerberos, cracking, offline.

items.title: Targeted Kerberoasting via writable servicePrincipalName
items.description: If you hold write access to a target user, set a temporary SPN on the account, Kerberoast it, then remove the SPN. This converts an ACL primitive into a crackable hash for any user. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You successfully add an SPN, obtain a TGS hash, and clean up the SPN afterward. Common tags: kerberos, targeted, acl-abuse.

Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: You authenticate successfully using AES keys against an AES-only target. Common tags: pth, aes, kerberos.
